channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-18838] stat ahead panic at _atomic_dec_and_lock()
item.description:
The Janitor testing on racer slow failed and crash as follows:

https://knox.linuxhacker.ru/crashdb_ui_external.py.cgi?newid=69679

Hardware name: Red Hat KVM, BIOS 1.16.0-3.module_el8.7.0+1218+f626c2ff 04/01/2014
task: ffff8802e7a59280 ti: ffff88031be08000 task.ti: ffff88031be08000
RIP: 0010:[<ffffffff813f502c>] [<ffffffff813f502c>] _atomic_dec_and_lock+0xc/0x70
[<ffffffffa11048a1>] ll_statahead_thread+0xe01/0x22b0 [lustre]
[<ffffffff817e320e>] ? _raw_spin_lock+0x1e/0x20
[<ffffffff810dbb51>] ? put_prev_entity+0x31/0x400
[<ffffffff814119f9>] ? do_raw_spin_unlock+0x49/0x90
[<ffffffff817e329e>] ? _raw_spin_unlock_irq+0xe/0x30
[<ffffffff810c834d>] ? finish_task_switch+0x5d/0x1b0
[<ffffffffa1103aa0>] ? ll_statahead_handle.constprop.29+0x1c0/0x1c0 [lustre]
[<ffffffff810ba114>] kthread+0xe4/0xf0
[<ffffffff810ba030>] ? kthread_create_on_node+0x140/0x140
[<ffffffff817ede5d>] ret_from_fork_nospec_begin+0x7/0x21
[<ffffffff810ba030>] ? kthread_create_on_node+0x140/0x140

The panic line of code:

static inline void ll_sax_put(struct inode *dir,
			      struct ll_statahead_context *ctx)
{
	struct ll_inode_info *lli = ll_i2info(dir);

	if (atomic_dec_and_lock(&ctx->sax_refcount, &lli->lli_sa_lock)) { ===> panic
		LASSERT(list_empty(&ctx->sax_sai_list));
		lli->lli_sai = NULL;

status.description: The issue is open and ready for the assignee to start work on it.

comments.comment:
"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/64093
Subject: LU-18838: llite: Debug NULL ctx
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 5ff56911562ee1eb5ea1148c0e44fd78a3d68475

Question & Quick Answer

Q: Can dentry->d_inode become NULL between dget(dentry) and dput(dentry)?
A: Yes, it can. dget() only guarantees the dentry object itself won't be freed; it does not guarantee the stability of its association with an inode.

What dget() Actually Protects

The function dget(dentry) performs a single crucial task: it increments the dentry's reference count (d_count), preventing the memory of the dentry object itself from being deallocated until a corresponding dput() is called .

It explicitly does NOT protect :
• The dentry->d_inode pointer from becoming NULL.

• The inode pointed to by d_inode from being disassociated or freed.

• The dentry's state (e.g., from "positive" to "negative").

The lifetime of the dentry object and its association with an inode are managed independently.

Scenario: How d_inode Can Become NULL

A typical race condition involves two threads operating on the same path :

Thread A (Your Code):

  1. 1. Holds a pointer to a dentry (e.g., from a lookup).
  2. 2. Calls dget(dentry) to grab a reference.
  3. 3. Intends to access dentry->d_inode later.

Thread B (Concurrent Operation):

  1. 1. Performs a delete operation (e.g., unlink) on the same file.
  2. 2. This triggers a call to d_delete(dentry) in the kernel.
  3. 3. If certain conditions are met (e.g., the dentry's reference count is 1 at the start of the delete operation), the kernel calls dentry_unlink_inode(dentry). The key function here is __d_clear_type_and_inode(dentry), which directly sets dentry->d_inode = NULL .

Result for Thread A:

When Thread A checks dentry->d_inode after this sequence, it will find it is NULL, even though the dentry object itself remains valid. The dentry has transitioned from a "positive" state to a "negative" state .

Key Takeaway

This behavior is a normal and intentional part of the VFS design. You must always assume that dentry->d_inode can become NULL after dget(). The dget() function protects the dentry's existence, not its connection to an inode.

Safe Usage Pattern

To safely access the inode, you should not rely solely on dget(). A common safe practice involves:

1. Using locks (like dentry->d_lock) to briefly check the d_inode pointer.
2. If the pointer is valid, obtaining a separate reference to the inode using ihold() or igrab().
3. Then releasing the dentry lock.

This pattern ensures that once you have a reference to the inode, it won't be freed prematurely.

Example pseudo-code:

struct inode *inode = NULL;

spin_lock(&dentry->d_lock);
if (dentry->d_inode) {
    inode = dentry->d_inode;
    ihold(inode); // Take a reference on the inode
}
spin_unlock(&dentry->d_lock);

if (inode) {
    // ... Safely use the inode ...
    iput(inode); // Release the inode reference
} else {
    // Handle negative dentry (file doesn't exist)
}

Using higher-level helper functions like d_inode() (which may include safety checks) is also recommended .

When is it Stable?

The association is stable only under broader synchronization mechanisms, such as when holding a parent directory's lock (e.g., i_rwsem), which prevents concurrent renames or deletions within that directory. This stability comes from the directory lock, not from dget().

The main panic is caused by the case that dentry->d_inode becomes NULL even after get() before final dput().

So I think the simple solution maybe your later patch:
https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/63645

Ugh, so I am trying to wrap my head around sai/sax in lli and everything is too convoluted, so I will write a comment here so we can have a bit of a discussion may be?

so ll_sax_put could in theory drop the refcount to 0 and that would lead to it cleaning lli->lli_sai and lli->lli_sax

the lli is taken from an externally-passed inode which potentially could have aboslutely nothing to do with the one initial statahead was called for I think? (e.g. statahead kicked off, parent invalidated and on revaliadtion another inode was pulled in for whatever reason)

The https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/63644 I have tries to address this by actually using internal inode pointer for the update - this takes care of the random inode lli clobbering, but highlights another issue:

it's all done with no locking whatsoever. In fact we can see the code everywhere goes to the great effort to call ll_sax_put outside of the lli_sai_lock somehow. Sure in some cases inside it would lock it, but why not make it mandatory to call with this lock called externally?

Similar situation with ll_sai_put.

The other problem here is: nothing appears to be actually pinning the inode, but if we actually pin it within sai (sax already pins the inode it attaches to) with the https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/63645 patch - we should be all good?

So with conversion like this we can make the sai_refcount non-atomic as all users are under lli_sa_lock with the exception of one call to __ll_sai_get() in ll_start_agl() that we'll need to cover probably at the callsite?

qian_wc what do you think?

 

Ah, I now see we fall into the same trap when we do it:

  1. the externally locked lli could be wrong one
  2. even if not as we do ll_sa[ix]_free() it unpins the inode and potentially frees the lli thta we still need to unlock, sigh

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/63645
Subject: LU-18838 llite: Add inode to struct ll_statahead_info
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 8963e9c23707059d3caf445ca0fba9ccbad66153

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/63644
Subject: LU-18838 lli: WIP Fix sax use race condition in ll_statahead_thread
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: f7c1d4ba6f3a812b934e08a62fc82f9989cc4410

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/63323
Subject: LU-18838 llite: fix race conditions in statahead causing NULL pointer crashes
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 47994ab3ffa33d0be3443a578df7cf649856502a

"Sohei Koyama <skoyama@ddn.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/62110
Subject: LU-18838 llite: fix race condition at putting sax
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 4d1a8f830e82de2bb9e2abe77a9e8b0d18c32bda

and yet another one:

[ 1690.809607] LustreError: 1456771:0:(statahead.c:1721:ll_deauthorize_statahead()) ASSERTION( lli->lli_stat_pid != 0 ) failed: 
[ 1690.809749] LustreError: 1456771:0:(statahead.c:1721:ll_deauthorize_statahead()) LBUG
[ 1690.809808] CPU: 0 PID: 1456771 Comm: lfs Tainted: G        W  O      -------- -  - 4.18.0 #2
[ 1690.809870] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.17.0-4.fc41 04/01/2014
[ 1690.809934] Call Trace:
[ 1690.810418]  dump_stack+0x6e/0xa0
[ 1690.810498]  lbug_with_loc.cold.3+0x5/0x4e [libcfs]
[ 1690.810548]  ll_deauthorize_statahead+0x2f8/0x3e0 [lustre]
[ 1690.810631]  ll_file_release+0x69b/0xa20 [lustre]
[ 1690.810704]  ? lock_acquire+0x1ab/0x310
[ 1690.810740]  __fput+0xc5/0x260
[ 1690.810776]  task_work_run+0x8a/0xc0
[ 1690.810811]  exit_to_usermode_loop+0xc5/0xd0
[ 1690.810852]  do_syscall_64+0x126/0x1a0
[ 1690.810885]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x4e/0xc3

other related issues:

this one in various forms happens quite a bit too

https://knox.linuxhacker.ru/crashdb_ui_external.py.cgi?newid=72722

 [ 8341.721043] LustreError: 30853:0:(statahead.c:2037:revalidate_statahead_dentry()) ASSERTION( sai == entry->se_sai ) failed:
[ 8341.723477] LustreError: 30853:0:(statahead.c:2037:revalidate_statahead_dentry()) LBUG
[ 8341.782623] CPU: 12 PID: 30853 Comm: ls Kdump: loaded Tainted: P           OE  ------------   3.10.0-7.9-debug #2
[ 8341.783741] Hardware name: Red Hat KVM, BIOS 1.16.0-3.module_el8.7.0+1218+f626c2ff 04/01/2014
[ 8341.784772] Call Trace:
[ 8341.785254]  [<ffffffff817d93f8>] dump_stack+0x19/0x1b
[ 8341.785851]  [<ffffffffa022dafd>] lbug_with_loc+0x4d/0xa0 [libcfs]
[ 8341.786425]  [<ffffffffa178e32b>] revalidate_statahead_dentry+0x14db/0x1620 [lustre]
[ 8341.787443]  [<ffffffff81068309>] ? kvm_sched_clock_read+0x9/0x20
[ 8341.787990]  [<ffffffff81035ec9>] ? sched_clock+0x9/0x10
[ 8341.788529]  [<ffffffff8110a245>] ? __raw_callee_save___pv_queued_spin_unlock_slowpath+0x15/0x24
[ 8341.789643]  [<ffffffff8110a274>] ? .slowpath+0x9/0x15
[ 8341.790184]  [<ffffffffa178eaf0>] ll_revalidate_statahead+0x80/0x360 [lustre]
[ 8341.790762]  [<ffffffffa1717256>] ll_revalidate_dentry+0x256/0x290 [lustre]
[ 8341.791324]  [<ffffffff81251fb4>] lookup_fast+0x1d4/0x220
[ 8341.865941]  [<ffffffff81255266>] do_last+0x3d6/0x1280

[ 8341.868208]  [<ffffffff814119f9>] ? do_raw_spin_unlock+0x49/0x90
[ 8341.868760]  [<ffffffff817e324e>] ? _raw_spin_unlock+0xe/0x20

[ 8341.872033]  [<ffffffff817edf55>] ? system_call_after_swapgs+0xa2/0x13a
[ 8341.872735] Kernel panic - not syncing: LBUG

https://knox.linuxhacker.ru/crashdb_ui_external.py.cgi?newid=68922

 [ 1592.623112] LustreError: 32300:0:(statahead.c:583:ll_sax_put()) ASSERTION( list_empty(&ctx->sax_sai_list) ) failed:
[ 1592.624438] LustreError: 32300:0:(statahead.c:583:ll_sax_put()) LBUG

[ 1592.626372] Hardware name: Red Hat KVM, BIOS 1.16.0-3.module_el8.7.0+1218+f626c2ff 04/01/2014

[ 1592.647090] Kernel panic - not syncing: LBUG

And so on.

 

Basically if you go to https://knox.linuxhacker.ru/crashdb_ui_external.py.cgi?search=statahead

and then ignore all the ones that have have obd_get_mod_rpc_slot as the crashing location, the rest are directly related or could be(just listing the IDs): 70579, 72764, 67850, 68523,
